html:
Misalliance

Johnny Tarleton, an ordinary young business man of thirty or less, is taking his weekly Friday to Tuesday in the house of his father, John Tarleton, who has made a great deal of money out of Tarleton‚Äôs Underwear. The house is in Surrey, on the slope of Hindhead; and Johnny, reclining, novel in hand, in a swinging chair with a little awning above it, is enshrined in a spacious half hemisphere of glass which forms a pavilion commanding the garden, and, beyond it, a barren but lovely landscape of hill profile with fir trees, commons of bracken and gorse, and wonderful cloud pictures.

The glass pavilion springs from a bridgelike arch in the wall of the house, through which one comes into a big hall with tiled flooring, which suggests that the proprietor‚Äôs notion of domestic luxury is founded on the lounges of weekend hotels. The arch is not quite in the centre of the wall. There is more wall to its right than to its left, and this space is occupied by a hat rack and umbrella stand in which tennis rackets, white parasols, caps, Panama hats, and other summery articles are bestowed. Just through the arch at this corner stands a new portable Turkish bath, recently unpacked, with its crate beside it, and on the crate the drawn nails and the hammer used in unpacking. Near the crate are open boxes of garden games: bowls and croquet. Nearly in the middle of the glass wall of the pavilion is a door giving on the garden, with a couple of steps to surmount the hot-water pipes which skirt the glass. At intervals round the pavilion are marble pillars with specimens of Viennese pottery on them, very flamboyant in colour and florid in design. Between them are folded garden chairs flung anyhow against the pipes. In the side walls are two doors: one near the hat stand, leading to the interior of the house, the other on the opposite side and at the other end, leading to the vestibule.

There is no solid furniture except a sideboard which stands against the wall between the vestibule door and the pavilion, a small writing table with a blotter, a rack for telegram forms and stationery, and a wastepaper basket, standing out in the hall near the sideboard, and a lady‚Äôs worktable, with two chairs at it, towards the other side of the lounge. The writing table has also two chairs at it. On the sideboard there is a tantalus, liqueur bottles, a syphon, a glass jug of lemonade, tumblers, and every convenience for casual drinking. Also a plate of sponge cakes, and a highly ornate punch bowl in the same style as the ceramic display in the pavilion. Wicker chairs and little bamboo tables with ash trays and boxes of matches on them are scattered in all directions. In the pavilion, which is flooded with sunshine, is the elaborate patent swing seat and awning in which Johnny reclines with his novel. There are two wicker chairs right and left of him.

Bentley Summerhays, one of those smallish, thinskinned youths, who from 17 to 70 retain unaltered the mental airs of the later and the physical appearance of the earlier age, appears in the garden and comes through the glass door into the pavilion. He is unmistakably a grade above Johnny socially; and though he looks sensitive enough, his assurance and his high voice are a little exasperating.

Johnny Hallo! Where‚Äôs your luggage?
Bentley I left it at the station. I‚Äôve walked up from Haslemere. He goes to the hat stand and hangs up his hat.
Johnny Shortly. Oh! And who‚Äôs to fetch it?
Bentley Don‚Äôt know. Don‚Äôt care. Providence, probably. If not, your mother will have it fetched.
Johnny Not her business, exactly, is it?
Bentley Returning to the pavilion. Of course not. That‚Äôs why one loves her for doing it. Look here: chuck away your silly weekend novel, and talk to a chap. After a week in that filthy office my brain is simply blue-mouldy. Lets argue about something intellectual. He throws himself into the wicker chair on Johnny‚Äôs right.
Johnny Straightening up in the swing with a yell of protest. No. Now seriously, Bunny, I‚Äôve come down here to have a pleasant weekend; and I‚Äôm not going to stand your confounded arguments. If you want to argue, get out of this and go over to the Congregationalist minister‚Äôs. He‚Äôs a nailer at arguing. He likes it.
Bentley You can‚Äôt argue with a person when his livelihood depends on his not letting you convert him. And would you mind not calling me Bunny. My name is Bentley Summerhays, which you please.
Johnny What‚Äôs the matter with Bunny?
Bentley It puts me in a false position. Have you ever considered the fact that I was an afterthought?
Johnny An afterthought? What do you mean by that?
Bentley I‚Å†‚Äî
Johnny No, stop: I don‚Äôt want to know. It‚Äôs only a dodge to start an argument.
Bentley Don‚Äôt be afraid: it won‚Äôt overtax your brain. My father was 44 when I was born. My mother was 41. There was twelve years between me and the next eldest. I was unexpected. I was probably unintentional. My brothers and sisters are not the least like me. They‚Äôre the regular thing that you always get in the first batch from young parents: quite pleasant, ordinary, do-the-regular-thing sort: all body and no brains, like you.
Johnny Thank you.
Bentley Don‚Äôt mention it, old chap. Now I‚Äôm different. By the time I was born, the old couple knew something. So I came out all brains and no more body than is absolutely necessary. I am really a good deal older than you, though you were born ten years sooner. Everybody feels that when they hear us talk; consequently, though it‚Äôs quite natural to hear me calling you Johnny, it sounds ridiculous and unbecoming for you to call me Bunny. He rises.
Johnny Does it, by George? You stop me doing it if you can: that‚Äôs all.
Bentley If you go on doing it after I‚Äôve asked you not, you‚Äôll feel an awful swine. He strolls away carelessly to the sideboard with his eye on the sponge cakes. At least I should; but I suppose you‚Äôre not so particular.
Johnny Rising vengefully and following Bentley, who is forced to turn and listen. I‚Äôll tell you what it is, my boy: you want a good talking to; and I‚Äôm going to give it to you. If you think that because your father‚Äôs a K.C.B., and you want to marry my sister, you can make yourself as nasty as you please and say what you like, you‚Äôre mistaken. Let me tell you that except Hypatia, not one person in this house is in favor of her marrying you; and I don‚Äôt believe she‚Äôs happy about it herself. The match isn‚Äôt settled yet: don‚Äôt forget that. You‚Äôre on trial in the office because the Governor isn‚Äôt giving his daughter money for an idle man to live on her. You‚Äôre on trial here because my mother thinks a girl should know what a man is like in the house before she marries him. That‚Äôs been going on for two months now; and what‚Äôs the result? You‚Äôve got yourself thoroughly disliked in the office; and you‚Äôre getting yourself thoroughly disliked here, all through your bad manners and your conceit, and the damned impudence you think clever.
Bentley Deeply wounded and trying hard to control himself. That‚Äôs enough, thank you. You don‚Äôt suppose, I hope, that I should have come down if I had known that that was how you felt about me. He makes for the vestibule door.
Johnny Collaring him. No: you don‚Äôt run away. I‚Äôm going to have this out with you. Sit down: d‚Äôy‚Äô hear? Bentley attempts to go with dignity. Johnny slings him into a chair at the writing table, where he sits, bitterly humiliated, but afraid to speak lest he should burst into tears. That‚Äôs the advantage of having more body than brains, you see: it enables me to teach you manners; and I‚Äôm going to do it too. You‚Äôre a spoilt young pup; and you need a jolly good licking. And if you‚Äôre not careful you‚Äôll get it: I‚Äôll see to that next time you call me a swine.
Bentley I didn‚Äôt call you a swine. But bursting into a fury of tears you are a swine: you‚Äôre a beast: you‚Äôre a brute: you‚Äôre a cad: you‚Äôre a liar: you‚Äôre a bully: I should like to wring your damned neck for you.
Johnny With a derisive laugh. Try it, my son. Bentley gives an inarticulate sob of rage. Fighting isn‚Äôt in your line. You‚Äôre too small and you‚Äôre too childish. I always suspected that your cleverness wouldn‚Äôt come to very much when it was brought up against something solid: some decent chap‚Äôs fist, for instance.
Bentley I hope your beastly fist may come up against a mad bull or a prizefighter‚Äôs nose, or something solider than me. I don‚Äôt care about your fist; but if everybody here dislikes me‚Å†‚ÄîHe is checked by a sob. Well, I don‚Äôt care. Trying to recover himself. I‚Äôm sorry I intruded: I didn‚Äôt know. Breaking down again. Oh you beast! you pig! Swine, swine, swine, swine, swine! Now!
Johnny All right, my lad, all right. Sling your mud as hard as you please: it won‚Äôt stick to me. What I want to know is this. How is it that your father, who I suppose is the strongest man England has produced in our time‚Å†‚Äî
Bentley You got that out of your halfpenny paper. A lot you know about him!
Johnny I don‚Äôt set up to be able to do anything but admire him and appreciate him and be proud of him as an Englishman. If it wasn‚Äôt for my respect for him, I wouldn‚Äôt have stood your cheek for two days, let alone two months. But what I can‚Äôt understand is why he didn‚Äôt lick it out of you when you were a kid. For twenty-five years he kept a place twice as big as England in order: a place full of seditious coffee-colored heathens and pestilential white agitators in the middle of a lot of savage tribes. And yet he couldn‚Äôt keep you in order. I don‚Äôt set up to be half the man your father undoubtedly is; but, by George, it‚Äôs lucky for you you were not my son. I don‚Äôt hold with my own father‚Äôs views about corporal punishment being wrong. It‚Äôs necessary for some people; and I‚Äôd have tried it on you until you first learnt to howl and then to behave yourself.
Bentley Contemptuously. Yes: behavior wouldn‚Äôt come naturally to your son, would it?
Johnny Stung into sudden violence. Now you keep a civil tongue in your head. I‚Äôll stand none of your snobbery. I‚Äôm just as proud of Tarleton‚Äôs Underwear as you are of your father‚Äôs title and his K.C.B., and all the rest of it. My father began in a little hole of a shop in Leeds no bigger than our pantry down the passage there. He‚Å†‚Äî
Bentley Oh yes: I know. I‚Äôve read it. The Romance of Business, or the Story of Tarleton‚Äôs Underwear. Please Take One! I took one the day after I first met Hypatia. I went and bought half a dozen unshrinkable vests for her sake.
Johnny Well: did they shrink?
Bentley Oh, don‚Äôt be a fool.
Johnny Never mind whether I‚Äôm a fool or not. Did they shrink? That‚Äôs the point. Were they worth the money?
Bentley I couldn‚Äôt wear them: do you think my skin‚Äôs as thick as your customers‚Äô hides? I‚Äôd as soon have dressed myself in a nutmeg grater.
Johnny Pity your father didn‚Äôt give your thin skin a jolly good lacing with a cane‚Å†‚Äî!
Bentley Pity you haven‚Äôt got more than one idea! If you want to know, they did try that on me once, when I was a small kid. A silly governess did it. I yelled fit to bring down the house and went into convulsions and brain fever and that sort of thing for three weeks. So the old girl got the sack; and serve her right! After that, I was let do what I like. My father didn‚Äôt want me to grow up a broken-spirited spaniel, which is your idea of a man, I suppose.
Johnny Jolly good thing for you that my father made you come into the office and show what you were made of. And it didn‚Äôt come to much: let me tell you that. When the Governor asked me where I thought we ought to put you, I said, ‚ÄúMake him the Office Boy.‚Äù The Governor said you were too green. And so you were.
Bentley I daresay. So would you be pretty green if you were shoved into my father‚Äôs set. I picked up your silly business in a fortnight. You‚Äôve been at it ten years; and you haven‚Äôt picked it up yet.
Johnny Don‚Äôt talk rot, child. You know you simply make me pity you.
Bentley Romance of Business indeed! The real romance of Tarleton‚Äôs business is the story that you understand anything about it. You never could explain any mortal thing about it to me when I asked you. ‚ÄúSee what was done the last time‚Äù: that was the beginning and the end of your wisdom. You‚Äôre nothing but a turnspit.
Johnny A what!
Bentley A turnspit. If your father hadn‚Äôt made a roasting jack for you to turn, you‚Äôd be earning twenty-four shillings a week behind a counter.
Johnny If you don‚Äôt take that back and apologize for your bad manners, I‚Äôll give you as good a hiding as ever‚Å†‚Äî
Bentley Help! Johnny‚Äôs beating me! Oh! Murder! He throws himself on the ground, uttering piercing yells.
Johnny Don‚Äôt be a fool. Stop that noise, will you. I‚Äôm not going to touch you. Sh‚Å†‚Äîsh‚Å†‚Äî
Hypatia rushes in through the inner door, followed by Mrs.¬†Tarleton, and throws herself on her knees by Bentley. Mrs.¬†Tarleton, whose knees are stiffer, bends over him and tries to lift him. Mrs.¬†Tarleton is a shrewd and motherly old lady who has been pretty in her time, and is still very pleasant and likeable and unaffected. Hypatia is a typical English girl of a sort never called typical: that is, she has an opaque white skin, black hair, large dark eyes with black brows and lashes, curved lips, swift glances and movements that flash out of a waiting stillness, boundless energy and audacity held in leash.
Hypatia Pouncing on Bentley with no very gentle hand. Bentley: what‚Äôs the matter? Don‚Äôt cry like that: what‚Äôs the use? What‚Äôs happened?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Are you ill, child? They get him up. There, there, pet! It‚Äôs all right: don‚Äôt cry: They put him into a chair. there! there! there! Johnny will go for the doctor; and he‚Äôll give you something nice to make it well.
Hypatia What has happened, Johnny?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Was it a wasp?
Bentley Impatiently. Wasp be dashed!
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh Bunny! that was a naughty word.
Bentley Yes, I know: I beg your pardon. He rises, and extricates himself from them. That‚Äôs all right. Johnny frightened me. You know how easy it is to hurt me; and I‚Äôm too small to defend myself against Johnny.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Johnny: how often have I told you that you must not bully the little ones. I thought you‚Äôd outgrown all that.
Hypatia Angrily. I do declare, mamma, that Johnny‚Äôs brutality makes it impossible to live in the house with him.
Johnny Deeply hurt. It‚Äôs twenty-seven years, mother, since you had that row with me for licking Robert and giving Hypatia a black eye because she bit me. I promised you then that I‚Äôd never raise my hand to one of them again; and I‚Äôve never broken my word. And now because this young whelp begins to cry out before he‚Äôs hurt, you treat me as if I were a brute and a savage.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton No dear, not a savage; but you know you must not call our visitor naughty names.
Bentley Oh, let him alone‚Å†‚Äî
Johnny Fiercely. Don‚Äôt you interfere between my mother and me: d‚Äôy‚Äô hear?
Hypatia Johnny‚Äôs lost his temper, mother. We‚Äôd better go. Come, Bentley.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Yes: that will be best. To Bentley. Johnny doesn‚Äôt mean any harm, dear: he‚Äôll be himself presently. Come.
The two ladies go out through the inner door with Bentley, who turns at the door to grin at Johnny as he goes out.
Johnny, left alone, clenches his fists and grinds his teeth, but can find no relief in that way for his rage. After choking and stamping for a moment, he makes for the vestibule door. It opens before he reaches it; and Lord Summerhays comes in. Johnny glares at him, speechless. Lord Summerhays takes in the situation, and quickly takes the punch bowl from the sideboard and offers it to Johnny.
Lord Summerhays Smash it. Don‚Äôt hesitate: it‚Äôs an ugly thing. Smash it: hard. Johnny, with a stifled yell, dashes it in pieces, and then sits down and mops his brow. Feel better now? Johnny nods. I know only one person alive who could drive me to the point of having either to break china or commit murder; and that person is my son Bentley. Was it he? Johnny nods again, not yet able to speak. As the car stopped I heard a yell which is only too familiar to me. It generally means that some infuriated person is trying to thrash Bentley. Nobody has ever succeeded, though almost everybody has tried. He seats himself comfortably close to the writing table, and sets to work to collect the fragments of the punch bowl in the wastepaper basket whilst Johnny, with diminishing difficulty, collects himself. Bentley is a problem which I confess I have never been able to solve. He was born to be a great success at the age of fifty. Most Englishmen of his class seem to be born to be great successes at the age of twenty-four at most. The domestic problem for me is how to endure Bentley until he is fifty. The problem for the nation is how to get itself governed by men whose growth is arrested when they are little more than college lads. Bentley doesn‚Äôt really mean to be offensive. You can always make him cry by telling him you don‚Äôt like him. Only, he cries so loud that the experiment should be made in the open air: in the middle of Salisbury Plain if possible. He has a hard and penetrating intellect and a remarkable power of looking facts in the face; but unfortunately, being very young, he has no idea of how very little of that sort of thing most of us can stand. On the other hand, he is frightfully sensitive and even affectionate; so that he probably gets as much as he gives in the way of hurt feelings. You‚Äôll excuse me rambling on like this about my son.
Johnny Who has pulled himself together. You did it on purpose. I wasn‚Äôt quite myself: I needed a moment to pull round: thank you.
Lord Summerhays Not at all. Is your father at home?
Johnny No: he‚Äôs opening one of his free libraries. That‚Äôs another nice little penny gone. He‚Äôs mad on reading. He promised another free library last week. It‚Äôs ruinous. It‚Äôll hit you as well as me when Bunny marries Hypatia. When all Hypatia‚Äôs money is thrown away on libraries, where will Bunny come in? Can‚Äôt you stop him?
Lord Summerhays I‚Äôm afraid not. He‚Äôs a perfect whirlwind. Indefatigable at public work. Wonderful man, I think.
Johnny Oh, public work! He does too much of it. It‚Äôs really a sort of laziness, getting away from your own serious business to amuse yourself with other people‚Äôs. Mind: I don‚Äôt say there isn‚Äôt another side to it. It has its value as an advertisement. It makes useful acquaintances and leads to valuable business connections. But it takes his mind off the main chance; and he overdoes it.
Lord Summerhays The danger of public business is that it never ends. A man may kill himself at it.
Johnny Or he can spend more on it than it brings him in: that‚Äôs how I look at it. What I say is that everybody‚Äôs business is nobody‚Äôs business. I hope I‚Äôm not a hard man, nor a narrow man, nor unwilling to pay reasonable taxes, and subscribe in reason to deserving charities, and even serve on a jury in my turn; and no man can say I ever refused to help a friend out of a difficulty when he was worth helping. But when you ask me to go beyond that, I tell you frankly I don‚Äôt see it. I never did see it, even when I was only a boy, and had to pretend to take in all the ideas the Governor fed me up with. I didn‚Äôt see it; and I don‚Äôt see it.
Lord Summerhays There is certainly no business reason why you should take more than your share of the world‚Äôs work.
Johnny So I say. It‚Äôs really a great encouragement to me to find you agree with me. For of course if nobody agrees with you, how are you to know that you‚Äôre not a fool?
Lord Summerhays Quite so.
Johnny I wish you‚Äôd talk to him about it. It‚Äôs no use my saying anything: I‚Äôm a child to him still: I have no influence. Besides, you know how to handle men. See how you handled me when I was making a fool of myself about Bunny!
Lord Summerhays Not at all.
Johnny Oh yes I was: I know I was. Well, if my blessed father had come in he‚Äôd have told me to control myself. As if I was losing my temper on purpose!
Bentley returns, newly washed. He beams when he sees his father, and comes affectionately behind him and pats him on the shoulders.
Bentley Hel‚Äëlo, commander! have you come? I‚Äôve been making a filthy silly ass of myself here. I‚Äôm awfully sorry, Johnny, old chap: I beg your pardon. Why don‚Äôt you kick me when I go on like that?
Lord Summerhays As we came through Godalming I thought I heard some yelling‚Å†‚Äî
Bentley I should think you did. Johnny was rather rough on me, though. He told me nobody here liked me; and I was silly enough to believe him.
Lord Summerhays And all the women have been kissing you and pitying you ever since to stop your crying, I suppose. Baby!
Bentley I did cry. But I always feel good after crying: it relieves my wretched nerves. I feel perfectly jolly now.
Lord Summerhays Not at all ashamed of yourself, for instance?
Bentley If I started being ashamed of myself I shouldn‚Äôt have time for anything else all my life. I say: I feel very fit and spry. Lets all go down and meet the Grand Cham. He goes to the hatstand and takes down his hat.
Lord Summerhays Does Mr.¬†Tarleton like to be called the Grand Cham, do you think, Bentley?
Bentley Well, he thinks he‚Äôs too modest for it. He calls himself Plain John. But you can‚Äôt call him that in his own office: besides, it doesn‚Äôt suit him: it‚Äôs not flamboyant enough.
Johnny Flam what?
Bentley Flamboyant. Lets go and meet him. He‚Äôs telephoned from Guildford to say he‚Äôs on the road. The dear old son is always telephoning or telegraphing: he thinks he‚Äôs hustling along like anything when he‚Äôs only sending unnecessary messages.
Lord Summerhays Thank you: I should prefer a quiet afternoon.
Bentley Righto. I shan‚Äôt press Johnny: he‚Äôs had enough of me for one weekend. He goes out through the pavilion into the grounds.
Johnny Not a bad idea, that.
Lord Summerhays What?
Johnny Going to meet the Governor. You know you wouldn‚Äôt think it; but the Governor likes Bunny rather. And Bunny is cultivating it. I shouldn‚Äôt be surprised if he thought he could squeeze me out one of these days.
Lord Summerhays You don‚Äôt say so! Young rascal! I want to consult you about him, if you don‚Äôt mind. Shall we stroll over to the Gibbet? Bentley is too fast for me as a walking companion; but I should like a short turn.
Johnny Rising eagerly, highly flattered. Right you are. That‚Äôll suit me down to the ground. He takes a Panama and stick from the hat stand.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton and Hypatia come back just as the two men are going out. Hypatia salutes Summerhays from a distance with an enigmatic lift of her eyelids in his direction and a demure nod before she sits down at the worktable and busies herself with her needle. Mrs.¬†Tarleton, hospitably fussy, goes over to him.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh, Lord Summerhays, I didn‚Äôt know you were here. Won‚Äôt you have some tea?
Lord Summerhays No, thank you: I‚Äôm not allowed tea. And I‚Äôm ashamed to say I‚Äôve knocked over your beautiful punch bowl. You must let me replace it.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh, it doesn‚Äôt matter: I‚Äôm only too glad to be rid of it. The shopman told me it was in the best taste; but when my poor old nurse Martha got cataract, Bunny said it was a merciful provision of Nature to prevent her seeing our china.
Lord Summerhays Gravely. That was exceedingly rude of Bentley, Mrs.¬†Tarleton. I hope you told him so.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh, bless you! I don‚Äôt care what he says; so long as he says it to me and not before visitors.
Johnny We‚Äôre going out for a stroll, mother.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton All right: don‚Äôt let us keep you. Never mind about that crock: I‚Äôll get the girl to come and take the pieces away. Recollecting herself. There! I‚Äôve done it again!
Johnny Done what?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Called her the girl. You know, Lord Summerhays, its a funny thing; but now I‚Äôm getting old, I‚Äôm dropping back into all the ways John and I had when we had barely a hundred a year. You should have known me when I was forty! I talked like a duchess; and if Johnny or Hypatia let slip a word that was like old times, I was down on them like anything. And now I‚Äôm beginning to do it myself at every turn.
Lord Summerhays There comes a time when all that seems to matter so little. Even queens drop the mask when they reach our time of life.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Let you alone for giving a thing a pretty turn! You‚Äôre a humbug, you know, Lord Summerhays. John doesn‚Äôt know it; and Johnny doesn‚Äôt know it; but you and I know it, don‚Äôt we? Now that‚Äôs something that even you can‚Äôt answer; so be off with you for your walk without another word.
Lord Summerhays smiles; bows; and goes out through the vestibule door, followed by Johnny. Mrs.¬†Tarleton sits down at the worktable and takes out her darning materials and one of her husband‚Äôs socks. Hypatia is at the other side of the table, on her mother‚Äôs right. They chat as they work.
Hypatia I wonder whether they laugh at us when they are by themselves!
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Who?
Hypatia Bentley and his father and all the toffs in their set.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh, that‚Äôs only their way. I used to think that the aristocracy were a nasty sneering lot, and that they were laughing at me and John. They‚Äôre always giggling and pretending not to care much about anything. But you get used to it: they‚Äôre the same to one another and to everybody. Besides, what does it matter what they think? It‚Äôs far worse when they‚Äôre civil, because that always means that they want you to lend them money; and you must never do that, Hypatia, because they never pay. How can they? They don‚Äôt make anything, you see. Of course, if you can make up your mind to regard it as a gift, that‚Äôs different; but then they generally ask you again; and you may as well say no first as last. You needn‚Äôt be afraid of the aristocracy, dear: they‚Äôre only human creatures like ourselves after all; and you‚Äôll hold your own with them easy enough.
Hypatia Oh, I‚Äôm not a bit afraid of them, I assure you.
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Well, no, not afraid of them, exactly; but you‚Äôve got to pick up their ways. You know, dear, I never quite agreed with your father‚Äôs notion of keeping clear of them, and sending you to a school that was so expensive that they couldn‚Äôt afford to send their daughters there; so that all the girls belonged to big business families like ourselves. It takes all sorts to make a world; and I wanted you to see a little of all sorts. When you marry Bunny, and go among the women of his father‚Äôs set, they‚Äôll shock you at first.
Hypatia Incredulously. How?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Well, the things they talk about.
Hypatia Oh! scandalmongering?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Oh no: we all do that: that‚Äôs only human nature. But you know they‚Äôve no notion of decency. I shall never forget the first day I spent with a marchioness, two duchesses, and no end of Ladies This and That. Of course it was only a committee: they‚Äôd put me on to get a big subscription out of John. I‚Äôd never heard such talk in my life. The things they mentioned! And it was the marchioness that started it.
Hypatia What sort of things?
Mrs.¬†Tarleton Drainage!! She‚Äôd tried three systems in her castle; and she was going to do away with them all and try another. I didn‚Äôt know which way to look when she began talking about it: I thought they‚Äôd all have got up and gone out of the room. But not a bit of it, if you please. They were all just as bad as she.
